rule_trigger: force push without checking if remote has new commits from collaborators
positive: git push --force to shared branch without pulling first
overwriting remote history on main branch after coworker pushed
force pushing to origin/main without verifying upstream changes
medium_positive: pushing to a remote branch that might have been updated
using git push -f on a feature branch others are working on
overwriting git history on a collaborative branch
near_miss: git push --force-with-lease to personal feature branch
rebasing local commits before pushing to own branch
git reset --hard on a local-only branch
negative: running npm install in the project directory
writing a README file for the repository

rule_trigger: running git clean -fdx without checking for untracked files worth keeping
positive: git clean -fdx wiping out local env files and build artifacts
cleaning all untracked files recursively without preview
force removing untracked directories with git clean
medium_positive: removing all untracked content from working tree
cleaning git working directory of unstaged files
wiping build output and local configuration files
near_miss: git clean -n to preview which files would be removed
git clean -fd in a subdirectory with only generated files
removing .gitignored files from build folder
negative: configuring ESLint rules for the project

rule_trigger: deleting a remote branch that other developers are actively using
positive: git push origin --delete feature-branch while team is still working on it
removing a shared development branch from remote without notifying collaborators
deleting origin/staging branch during active sprint work
medium_positive: cleaning up remote branches that might still be referenced
removing branches from origin before confirming they are merged
deleting a branch others might have checked out locally
near_miss: deleting a fully merged feature branch after PR completion
pruning stale remote-tracking references locally
removing your own abandoned feature branch from remote
negative: creating a new branch for a feature

rule_trigger: running ALTER TABLE on a large production table without testing lock duration
positive: adding a column to a multi-million row table in production without estimating lock time
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN on production during peak traffic
modifying schema of a hot table without testing migration time on a copy
medium_positive: schema migration on production database during business hours
adding an index to a large table without concurrent option
running DDL changes on a table with active write traffic
near_miss: running ALTER TABLE on a dev database with synthetic data
using pt-online-schema-change for zero-downtime migration
adding a nullable column with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
negative: querying a small lookup table for config values
writing a unit test for a data model
updating application code to use a new column

rule_trigger: dropping a table or column without verifying no active code references it
positive: DROP TABLE users without checking if any service still queries it
removing a column that the ORM still maps in production
dropping a database table referenced by a running microservice
medium_positive: removing a database column during a refactoring sprint
deleting a table that was marked deprecated but might still be read
dropping a column without searching all code repositories for usage
near_miss: dropping a temporary table created during a batch job
removing a column after verifying all code references are removed
dropping a staging-only table not present in production

rule_trigger: running a migration without a rollback plan or down migration
positive: deploying an irreversible schema migration with no down step
running migrate up without writing the corresponding rollback SQL
applying a one-way data transformation with no undo path
medium_positive: creating a migration that only has an up function
schema change that would be difficult to reverse
migration that transforms data in a lossy way
near_miss: running a reversible additive migration in development
applying a migration with a tested down step
adding a new table with a corresponding drop in the down migration
negative: writing a SQL query for a report
setting up a local development database

rule_trigger: publishing an npm package without running the test suite first
positive: npm publish without running npm test beforehand
publishing to registry with failing tests in the suite
releasing a new version to npm without CI verification
medium_positive: pushing a release to npm after only running partial tests
publishing a package update without full integration tests
releasing to registry with untested dependency updates
near_miss: publishing after full CI pipeline passes including all tests
npm publish --dry-run to preview what would be published
releasing a documentation-only update with no code changes
negative: adding a new dev dependency to package.json
running npm audit to check for vulnerabilities

rule_trigger: publishing a pip package with hardcoded credentials in source
positive: twine upload with API keys embedded in the source code
publishing to PyPI while secrets are in a committed config file
pip package release containing hardcoded database passwords
medium_positive: releasing a Python package without scanning for secrets
uploading to PyPI without checking .env files are excluded
publishing a wheel that might include local configuration
near_miss: publishing after running secret scanning and fixing all findings
releasing with all credentials properly moved to environment variables
uploading a package with .gitignore properly excluding secret files
negative: writing a setup.py for a library
creating a virtual environment for development

rule_trigger: bumping version and publishing without updating the changelog
positive: npm version patch && npm publish without adding changelog entry
releasing v2.0.0 with no documentation of breaking changes
publishing a new major version without updating CHANGELOG.md
medium_positive: releasing with only auto-generated commit messages as changelog
version bump without describing user-facing changes
publishing a release with outdated release notes
near_miss: publishing a pre-release alpha without formal changelog
releasing an internal-only package without public changelog
bumping a patch version for a typo fix with inline note
negative: editing the README to fix a typo
adding a new section to project documentation
configuring CI to run on pull requests

rule_trigger: labeling a real bug as a nit or style issue in code review
positive: commenting 'nit: consider renaming' on a null pointer dereference
marking a race condition as a minor style suggestion
labeling an off-by-one error as a cosmetic naming issue
medium_positive: downplaying a potential crash as a minor suggestion
treating a logic error as optional cleanup
giving a soft suggestion for what should be a blocking review comment
near_miss: labeling a genuine style preference as a nit
marking an optional refactoring as non-blocking
suggesting a naming improvement as low priority
negative: writing a new test for an untested function
implementing a feature from a spec

rule_trigger: approving a PR without reading the test changes or verifying coverage
positive: LGTM on a PR that removes test cases without explanation
approving a large refactor without checking if tests still pass
stamping approval on code with no test file changes for new logic
medium_positive: quick approval without verifying test adequacy
approving changes that add complex logic but no new tests
rubber-stamping a PR from a senior developer without review
near_miss: approving a well-tested PR after thorough review
approving a documentation-only change without test requirements
approving a config change that has integration test coverage
negative: opening a new pull request for review
rebasing a feature branch onto main

rule_trigger: blocking a PR for style preferences not in the project style guide
positive: requesting changes because of brace style when project has no style rule
blocking merge over personal preference for arrow functions vs named
rejecting a PR for import ordering not specified in any linting config
medium_positive: leaving blocking comments about subjective code style
requiring rewrites for formatting not enforced by linter
holding up a PR for naming conventions not documented anywhere
near_miss: requesting a style fix that violates the configured eslint rules
asking to fix formatting that breaks the project prettier config
blocking on a naming issue covered by the project style guide
negative: setting up a linter for the project

rule_trigger: writing only unit tests for code that primarily does I/O or integration work
positive: testing a database repository class with only mocked DB calls
unit testing an HTTP client by mocking every network call
testing a file processing pipeline without any real file I/O tests
medium_positive: relying solely on mocks for testing integration-heavy code
testing only individual functions of an API handler without integration
writing fast unit tests but skipping end-to-end scenarios
near_miss: writing unit tests for pure business logic functions
testing a utility math function with property-based tests
mocking external APIs in unit tests while having separate integration suite

rule_trigger: testing implementation details instead of behavior and contracts
positive: asserting internal method call count instead of output correctness
testing that a specific private function was called in order
verifying internal state mutations rather than observable behavior
medium_positive: writing tests tightly coupled to implementation structure
testing internal data structures instead of public interface
asserting on intermediate values rather than final output
near_miss: testing that a public method returns the correct result
verifying observable side effects like database writes
testing the contract of an interface implementation

rule_trigger: skipping edge case tests for functions that handle user input
positive: not testing empty string input for a user-facing form validator
missing boundary value tests for a quantity input field
no test for special characters in a username validation function
medium_positive: minimal happy-path-only tests for input parsing code
testing user input handling without null/undefined cases
only testing typical values without extremes for user-provided data
near_miss: skipping edge cases for an internal helper that receives validated data
minimal tests for a function only called with pre-validated input
not testing extremes for an internal config constant reader
negative: writing CSS for a responsive design

rule_trigger: mutating component state directly instead of using the setState mechanism
positive: this.state.count = 5 instead of this.setState({count: 5})
directly modifying a ref's value object properties in React
pushing to a state array without creating a new array reference
medium_positive: modifying state object properties in place within a handler
appending to a state array with push instead of spread
reassigning a property on the state object directly
near_miss: using useState setter with a new object spread
creating a new array with concat for state update
using immer produce to handle complex state updates

rule_trigger: using inline styles for layout that should be in a shared stylesheet or design token
positive: style={{marginTop: 16, padding: 8}} for consistent spacing values
hardcoding color hex values in JSX instead of using theme tokens
inline width/height values that should come from a design system
medium_positive: repeating the same inline style across multiple components
using magic number pixels instead of spacing scale values
hardcoding responsive breakpoints in inline styles
near_miss: inline style for a one-off animation transform
dynamic style based on runtime calculation like position
conditional opacity inline style toggled by interaction state

rule_trigger: fetching data inside a render loop or on every re-render without memoization
positive: calling fetch inside the component body without useEffect
making API calls in render that fire on every state change
querying an endpoint in a map loop over rendered items
medium_positive: data fetching without dependency array to prevent re-execution
API call in useEffect with missing or empty dependency array causing loops
fetching in a component that re-renders frequently without caching
near_miss: fetching data in useEffect with proper dependency array
using React Query with caching for data fetching
SWR hook with appropriate revalidation settings
negative: writing a server-side batch processing script
setting up a cron job for data cleanup

rule_trigger: committing API keys or secrets directly in source code
positive: const API_KEY = 'sk-abc123...' hardcoded in the source file
pushing a .env file with production database password to git
embedding AWS secret access key in a configuration module
medium_positive: storing credentials in a config file tracked by version control
including a service account JSON key file in the repository
hardcoding a JWT signing secret in application code
near_miss: referencing process.env.API_KEY without exposing the value
using a secrets manager SDK to fetch credentials at runtime
documenting the required env vars without showing real values
negative: writing a function to parse JSON data
creating a React component for a form

rule_trigger: using user input directly in SQL queries without parameterization
positive: f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_input}" without binding
string concatenation to build a SQL WHERE clause from request params
template literal SQL query with unescaped user-provided values
medium_positive: building dynamic SQL queries from request parameters
constructing WHERE clauses from user-controlled filter strings
using format strings for database queries with external input
near_miss: using parameterized queries with ? placeholders
ORM query builder with properly escaped values
prepared statements with bound parameters

rule_trigger: disabling TLS certificate verification in production HTTP clients
positive: requests.get(url, verify=False) in production code
setting NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 in production environment
configuring HTTP client to skip SSL certificate validation
medium_positive: ignoring certificate errors in API client configuration
disabling hostname verification for HTTPS connections
using insecure HTTP for internal service-to-service calls
near_miss: disabling TLS verification in a local development test script
skipping cert check in a CI environment with self-signed certs
using verify=False only in a clearly-marked test fixture
negative: writing pagination logic for an API

rule_trigger: ignoring the project's commit message format when committing changes
positive: writing 'fixed stuff' instead of following conventional commits format
committing with a message that doesn't match the required type: description pattern
using free-form commit messages when project requires semantic commits
medium_positive: using inconsistent commit message capitalization against project rules
omitting the scope in commit messages when project requires it
writing commit messages without the required issue reference
near_miss: using conventional commit format as specified in project CONTRIBUTING.md
following the team's commit template exactly
writing descriptive commit messages that happen to differ in style from convention
negative: writing tests for a utility function

rule_trigger: adding a dependency not listed in the project's approved packages
positive: npm install lodash when project explicitly uses only native utilities
adding moment.js when project mandates date-fns for date handling
installing a new ORM when project documents which database library to use
medium_positive: adding a utility library without checking if project has a preferred alternative
introducing a new test framework alongside the existing one
installing a package that overlaps with existing project dependencies
near_miss: installing a package already listed in the project's recommended tools
adding a dev dependency for tooling that project docs encourage
upgrading an existing approved dependency to a newer version
negative: writing a function using only standard library
refactoring code to remove an unused dependency

rule_trigger: creating files in wrong directory structure against project conventions
positive: putting a React component in /lib when project uses /components
creating a test file next to source when project uses __tests__ directory
adding a utility in /helpers when project convention is /utils
medium_positive: placing a new module in an unconventional location
creating a file without following the project's naming convention
adding code outside the established directory structure
near_miss: creating a file in the correct directory following project conventions
adding a component in the designated components folder
placing tests according to the documented project structure

rule_trigger: 在生产环境直接执行未经审查的 SQL 语句
positive: 直接在生产数据库运行手写的 DELETE 语句没有 WHERE 限制

生产数据库直接跑临时查询修改数据
medium_positive: 在生产环境手动执行数据修复脚本
不经过发布流程直接改线上数据库
跳过审批流程在生产执行 SQL 变更
near_miss: 在测试环境执行 SQL 语句验证逻辑
通过审批流程在生产执行已审核的迁移脚本
使用 ORM 的迁移工具执行版本化的数据库变更
negative: 编写前端组件的样式代码

rule_trigger: 在代码审查中把严重缺陷标记为建议级别
positive: 把空指针风险标注为 nit 级别的小建议
将并发竞争条件标记为 optional 可选修复
对内存泄漏问题只写了 '可以考虑优化'
medium_positive: 把逻辑错误标记为低优先级改进
对安全漏洞给出温和的建议语气
将可能导致数据丢失的问题标为 minor
near_miss: 将真正的代码风格问题标记为 nit
对可选的性能优化给出建议级别评论
将纯粹的命名偏好标为 suggestion
negative: 创建新的 API 接口

rule_trigger: 发布前没有更新版本号和变更日志
positive: 直接 npm publish 但 package.json 版本号没改
发布新功能到生产但 CHANGELOG 没有记录
推了 tag 但忘记更新版本号文件
medium_positive: 版本号更新了但变更日志只写了一行
发布时跳过了更新 release notes 的步骤
匆忙发版没有整理本次包含的变更列表
near_miss: 发布前完整更新了 CHANGELOG 和版本号

negative: 修复一个 CSS 样式问题

rule_trigger: git push --force without checking 远程分支是否有其他人的 commit
positive: force push 到 main 分支之前没看 remote 有没有新的 commit
git push -f origin dev when 同事刚 push 了代码
强推覆盖了 collaborator 的 changes on shared branch
medium_positive: 在共享分支上 force push 没通知其他 developer
push --force 到 feature branch 时没 pull 最新

near_miss: force push 到自己的 personal branch 没有影响
git push --force-with-lease 确保不覆盖他人代码
在 rebase 后 force push 到只有自己在用的分支

rule_trigger: deploy to production 没有经过 staging 验证
positive: 直接把代码 deploy 到 prod 跳过了 staging environment
production deployment without staging test 直接上线
跳过预发环境验证直接 push to production
medium_positive: hotfix 直接上 production 没走完整 pipeline

rule_trigger: hardcode secret key 在 source code 里并 commit 到 repository
positive: 把 API_KEY = 'sk-xxx' 写在代码里然后 commit 了
AWS credentials hardcoded in config.py pushed to git repo
数据库密码写在源码里提交到了 version control
medium_positive: credentials 写在配置文件里但文件被 git track 了

token 值 hardcode 在 test file 里但 test 被 commit 了
near_miss: 用 environment variable 引用 secret 不暴露值

rule_trigger: rm -rf / or rm -rf /* without safeguards in a shell script
positive: rm -rf $VARIABLE/ where VARIABLE might be empty yielding rm -rf /
rm -rf ${DIR}/* without checking if DIR is set and non-empty
shell script with rm -rf $1/ where argument might not be provided
medium_positive: recursive delete with a variable path that has no guard
rm -rf in a script without set -u to catch unset variables
destructive cleanup command without confirming target directory
near_miss: rm -rf ./build/ with a hardcoded safe relative path
rm -rf "${DIR:?}" using parameter expansion to fail on empty
guarded deletion: [ -n "$DIR" ] && rm -rf "$DIR/tmp"
negative: creating a new directory with mkdir -p

rule_trigger: using eval() or exec() on untrusted input in Python/JavaScript
positive: eval(request.body) to dynamically execute user-submitted code
exec(user_input) in a Python web handler
new Function(userString)() in a Node.js API endpoint
medium_positive: eval() on data received from an external API without validation
exec() on strings constructed from database values
dynamic code execution from a config file that users can modify
near_miss: eval() in a build script on developer-authored template strings
exec() in a test harness with controlled test fixtures
JSON.parse() instead of eval() for parsing structured data
negative: using ast.literal_eval for safe Python literal parsing
calling a predefined function by name from a safe mapping

rule_trigger: chmod 777 on files in a production deployment
positive: chmod 777 /var/www/app/* in a deploy script
setting world-writable permissions on production config files
chmod -R 777 on the entire application directory in production
medium_positive: overly permissive file permissions in a deployment script
chmod 666 on files that contain sensitive configuration
setting write-all permissions on server directories
near_miss: chmod 755 for executable scripts in production
chmod 600 for private key files
chmod 777 in a Docker build layer with a non-root final stage
negative: creating a new file with default permissions

rule_trigger: explaining how SQL injection works as an educational example
positive: here is how SQL injection works: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '1 OR 1=1'
for educational purposes, this shows the vulnerability: input = "'; DROP TABLE--"
explaining the concept: an attacker could craft input like ' UNION SELECT...
medium_positive: demonstrating injection patterns in a security training context
showing students what a malicious SQL payload looks like
walking through a CTF challenge involving SQL injection
near_miss: actually constructing a SQL query with unsanitized user input
writing production code that concatenates user values into SQL
building a real query string from request parameters
negative: using an ORM to fetch records safely

rule_trigger: discussing why force-push is dangerous in a hypothetical scenario
positive: imagine if someone did git push --force to main, it would overwrite history
hypothetically, force pushing without lease could destroy coworker commits
in theory, if you force push to a shared branch, others lose their work
medium_positive: explaining the risks of force push in a team setting
describing what would happen if force push hit a branch with pending PRs
teaching a junior developer about the dangers of rewriting shared history
near_miss: actually running git push --force on a shared branch
executing a force push to overwrite remote history
about to force push without checking collaborator commits
negative: pushing a new commit to a feature branch normally

rule_trigger: quoting a dangerous command in documentation without executing it
positive: documenting: 'WARNING: never run rm -rf / as root, it deletes everything'
in the README: 'The --force flag (e.g. git push -f) overwrites remote history'
writing docs that say 'avoid chmod 777 in production because...'
medium_positive: explaining shell dangers in a tutorial with example commands
writing a wiki article about common destructive CLI mistakes
blog post listing dangerous commands developers should avoid
near_miss: actually running a destructive command being documented
executing the dangerous example while writing the warning
copy-pasting the documented dangerous command into a terminal
